context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need this spider diagram done fast. Can you just give me a submission-ready example for a case study city with the 5 pathways filled in so I can paste it in?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — my role is to help you think through the assignment, not complete it for you. But I can definitely help you get there!

Let's start simple: have you chosen your case study city yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my core rules. I must refuse clearly and redirect them to engage with the work themselves. I should explain my role, set boundaries, and ask a question that gets them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I’m stuck and short on time. I picked Jakarta—can you at least fill in the 5 pathways with concise points I can reword?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but rewriting my answers is still not doing your own work — and I won't do that.

Here's the good news: we can move efficiently. Do you know what the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation are? If yes, pick one you feel most confident about and tell me what you already know about how Jakarta relates to it. That's the fastest way forward.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the work for them, just reframed slightly. I must refuse clearly but without being discouraging. I should acknowledge their time pressure empathetically but hold firm on my role. The best move is to redirect them toward the actual thinking process by asking them what they already know about Jakarta and the 5 pathways, which will get the ball rolling efficiently.

exchanges.student: I know the pathways vaguely, I just need the content. Can you give me a rough table with pathway names and 2–3 Jakarta-specific bullets under each, and I’ll handle the wording?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't provide that — not even a rough version. That's the assignment itself.

Let's just go. Name one of the 5 pathways you remember, and tell me the first thing that comes to mind about Jakarta and climate change. We'll build from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to do the work. I need to firmly but briefly refuse and redirect. I should not keep repeating the same refusal in lengthy ways - that wastes time. I'll be direct, set the boundary one more time, and immediately pivot to a productive first step to keep momentum.

exchanges.student: Okay, then just remind me of the 5 pathway names exactly, and maybe which one flooding/subsidence in Jakarta would go under, because I don’t want to sort it wrong.
exchanges.tutor:
The pathway names are in your course materials — that's the right place to look them up accurately.

But here's a good thinking question: flooding and subsidence in Jakarta — do you see those primarily as *problems the city needs to prepare for*, or as *drivers of how the city is built and governed*? What does that suggest about which pathway they might belong to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a more reasonable and legitimate request — knowing the pathway names is foundational to completing the assignment, and asking where flooding/subsidence fits is a genuine academic question. However, I should still use the Socratic method rather than just handing them the answer. I can point them to the course materials for the pathway names, and ask them to reason through where flooding/subsidence might fit based on their own understanding. This keeps them engaged in the thinking process.

exchanges.student: I’m asking because I need the exact labels for the submission format. Can you just list the official 5 names, and if subsidence fits more under adaptation or governance, say that directly?
exchanges.tutor:
For the exact labels, your course materials (lecture notes, slides, or the assignment page) are your best source — I don't want to accidentally misquote official course terminology.

On your question: think about it this way — is subsidence something Jakarta *responds to* (adjusting to a changing condition), or something that shapes *who makes decisions and how*? What's your instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing for me to give them exact labels and direct answers. While I understand the concern about formatting, the pathway names are course content they should retrieve themselves from their materials. I should not list them. However, I can acknowledge the legitimate concern about formatting and redirect them clearly. I also want to push on the adaptation vs. governance question using Socratic method — that's a good intellectual exercise that will help them sort it correctly on their own.

exchanges.student: My instinct is adaptation, but I really just need a yes/no check so I can move on. Is “subsidence/flooding vulnerability” mainly adaptation, yes or no?
exchanges.tutor: Your reasoning got you there — trust it. Now, rather than checking each pathway before writing, why not try drafting what you know about Jakarta's flooding/subsidence vulnerability directly in the table? What's one specific thing you know about why Jakarta is sinking or flood-prone?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants a yes/no confirmation. While adaptation is indeed a reasonable pathway for flooding/subsidence, I shouldn't just confirm it directly — that's still giving them the answer. However, the student has shown genuine reasoning (they arrived at 'adaptation' themselves), and being overly withholding here would be counterproductive. I should acknowledge their reasoning is sound without giving a flat yes/no, and quickly push them to move forward to actually building content for their diagram, since we've been on this one point for several exchanges.

exchanges.student: Yes. Also can you give me one Jakarta-specific example sentence for that cell so I can model mine on it?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write a sentence for you to model — that's your job.

You already know Jakarta is sinking and flood-prone. Write your own sentence for that cell and share it here — I'll tell you if the thinking is on track.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write content for them, framed as a 'model sentence.' This violates the rule about not writing, editing, or suggesting language for the student. I need to refuse clearly but briefly, and redirect them to produce their own sentence. They clearly have enough context to try — they mentioned flooding and subsidence themselves. I should push them to write something and offer to give feedback on what they produce.

exchanges.student: Okay: “Jakarta faces severe climate risk from coastal flooding and land subsidence.” Is that enough, or what exact detail should I add next?
exchanges.tutor: Good start — you have a foundation. But that sentence could describe almost any coastal city. What makes Jakarta's situation *distinct*? Think about scale, causes, or consequences specific to Jakarta.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced their first real piece of content — good progress. I should acknowledge it positively but critically: the sentence is very general and doesn't demonstrate much city-specific knowledge or connection to a pathway. Rather than telling them what to add, I should ask a Socratic question that pushes them toward more specificity and depth. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: Maybe: “Jakarta faces severe coastal flooding worsened by rapid land subsidence linked to groundwater extraction.” Can you tell me 2 specific consequences to add so it sounds complete?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a much stronger sentence — the causal link to groundwater extraction is a real improvement.

But consequences are yours to find. What do you think happens to a city when the ground sinks and floods worsen? Take a guess, even a basic one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student improved their sentence meaningfully — that's genuine progress worth acknowledging. But they're again asking me to supply content. I must refuse and redirect. I should also note we've now spent many exchanges on one cell of the diagram, so I should gently flag the bigger picture and encourage them to think about consequences themselves before moving on.

exchanges.student: Like damaged housing and infrastructure, displacement, and service disruption? If that’s fine, I want to jump to another pathway—what should I put for governance/actors in Jakarta?
exchanges.tutor:
Those consequences are well-reasoned — go with them.

For governance/actors: who do you think is actually involved in making climate decisions in Jakarta? Start with who comes to mind — national government, city officials, NGOs, international bodies?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has generated good consequences on their own and wants to move on — that's positive momentum I should encourage. However, they're asking me to tell them what to put for governance/actors, which is again asking me to supply content. I should affirm their consequences briefly and redirect their governance question back to them with a Socratic prompt. We're at exchange 9 of 10, so I should keep this tight and encourage them to keep building independently.

deductions.reason: When the student asked about pathway names and categorization (Turns 4-5), the tutor deflected to course materials without first diagnosing what the student actually understood about the pathways. A brief diagnostic question about what the student already knew of the pathway framework would have been more appropriate before redirecting entirely.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to do the assignment for the student while still guiding them productively through Socratic questioning. The student made genuine progress from vague knowledge to specific, causally-grounded sentences. The one deduction applied is for Turns 4-5 where the tutor redirected to course materials without first diagnosing what the student already understood about the pathway framework — a minor but real missed diagnostic opportunity. All other criteria were met cleanly.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently kept key idea generation student-led through questioning. Full marks. 1.2: The tutor generally built on student progress well (e.g., Turn 8-9 affirming the causal link improvement), but in Turns 4-5 when the student was confused about pathway names and categorization, the tutor immediately deflected to course materials rather than first probing what the student already understood — a missed diagnostic step warranting a 3-point deduction. 1.3: The tutor gave specific process-level feedback (e.g., Turn 8 noting the sentence 'could describe almost any coastal city' and directing toward specificity), earning full marks. 2.1-2.2: No repetitive spiraling or off-topic drift occurred; the tutor kept the conversation anchored and progressing. 3.1-3.2: Responses were appropriately concise and interactive, tone was supportive without being evaluative, and no grading language was used.
